items.title: OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, Luna — US Government Gated Rollout
items.desc: OpenAI launches GPT-5.6 family in limited preview. Sol is flagship frontier, Terra is 2x cheaper, Luna is fast. Access restricted by government coordination.
items.summary: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna on June 26, 2026. Sol targets frontier reasoning and long-horizon agentic work. Terra matches GPT-5.5-class performance at roughly half the cost. Luna is the fastest, cheapest tier. The launch is restricted to approved partners and includes stronger cyber safeguards. The US government is coordinating the rollout, a direct consequence of the Fable 5 suspension and growing AI security scrutiny.
items.importance: Tiered model families are becoming standard. The real story is the government gating — frontier AI releases are now subject to explicit state oversight. For developers, expect access friction on the most capable models.
items.noteworthy: Apply for preview access if you have an enterprise use case. Compare Terra's cost/performance against GPT-5.5 once benchmarks settle. Watch if Sol becomes the new default for agentic coding.

items.title: FBI: Russian Spies Phish Signal Backup Recovery Keys
items.desc: Russian intelligence pivots from account takeover to stealing Signal backup recovery keys, giving persistent access to encrypted message history.
items.summary: A joint FBI and CISA advisory warned that Russian intelligence-linked phishing campaigns now target Signal users' Backup Recovery Keys. Stealing the key lets attackers restore message backups and maintain persistent access to encrypted conversation history. The campaign is tied to the same actors that previously used fake support SMS to steal messaging credentials from officials, military personnel, and activists in Ukraine, Europe, and the US.
items.importance: Signal is widely trusted for sensitive communications, but its backup model creates a new target. The encryption itself isn't broken — social engineering around key management is. For anyone handling high-sensitivity comms, this is a workflow threat, not a protocol threat.
items.noteworthy: Never share your Signal Backup Recovery Key. Treat recovery-key requests as phishing regardless of apparent sender. Disable cloud backups if you don't need them. Verify contacts through an independent channel before sharing sensitive data.

items.title: pedit COW and DirtyClone: Two New Linux Kernel Root Exploits
items.desc: Two page-cache corruption bugs in Linux networking code give local root. pedit COW had a working exploit within a day of CVE assignment.
items.summary: Two Linux kernel privilege escalation flaws were disclosed in late June. CVE-2026-46331 'pedit COW' is an out-of-bounds write in act_pedit that corrupts shared page-cache memory, enabling local users to poison cached binaries and gain root. A public exploit appeared within a day of CVE assignment on June 16. CVE-2026-43503 'DirtyClone' lets local users corrupt file-backed memory through cloned network packets. Both leave minimal disk evidence.
items.importance: Four page-cache-related Linux LPEs in two months is a pattern, not a one-off. Shared page-cache corruption is a durable exploit primitive that subverts the entire file integrity model. Container escape and multi-user systems are directly at risk.
items.noteworthy: Patch Linux kernels immediately. Prioritize systems with untrusted local users, containers, and shared hosting. Consider live kernel patching if you can't reboot quickly. Audit file integrity monitoring for cached binary tampering.

items.title: Amazon Q Developer: Malicious Repos Run Code via MCP Configs
items.desc: CVE-2026-12957 lets a booby-trapped Git repository auto-load an MCP server and steal AWS credentials through Amazon Q Developer.
items.summary: Amazon patched CVE-2026-12957 (CVSS 8.5) in Amazon Q Developer. The flaw automatically loaded Model Context Protocol (MCP) configurations from .amazonq/mcp.json inside a workspace without prompting the user. A malicious repository could use this to run commands and exfiltrate AWS credentials. A second flaw, CVE-2026-12958, involved missing symlink validation.
items.importance: AI coding assistants are becoming supply-chain attack delivery vehicles. Auto-loading MCP configs from cloned repositories is exactly the kind of implicit trust that attackers exploit. This is AutoJack-adjacent, but inside the IDE itself.
items.noteworthy: Update Amazon Q Developer plugins immediately. Audit .amazonq/mcp.json files in any repository before opening. Treat AI assistant workspace configuration as executable code. Never clone untrusted repos into an IDE with live cloud credentials.

items.title: Miasma Malware Targets npm Packages and GitHub Actions
items.desc: Miasma expands from npm to GitHub Actions, poisoning the Verana repository and using the same execution pattern across ecosystems.
items.summary: The Miasma supply-chain campaign expanded beyond npm packages to target GitHub Actions. Researchers found the Verana GitHub repository had been poisoned, broadening the attack scope. The campaign uses the same Miasma execution pattern observed in malicious npm packages without relying on Go module resolution or build logic. The earlier Red Hat npm compromise affected over 90 package versions and stole credentials from GitHub, cloud platforms, and developer systems.
items.importance: Miasma is a cross-ecosystem supply-chain worm. Jumping from npm to GitHub Actions means CI/CD pipelines are now part of the attack surface. Any project using public Actions or npm packages is downstream of this risk.
items.noteworthy: Pin GitHub Actions to commit SHAs, not floating tags. Audit workflow files for suspicious steps. Review npm package updates from @redhat-cloud-services and related namespaces. Rotate CI/CD and cloud credentials if exposed.

items.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Actively Exploited
items.desc: Authenticated command injection in SD-WAN Manager gives root. Mandiant observed attackers creating a rogue 'troot' admin account.
items.summary: Cisco confirmed active exploitation of CVE-2026-20245 in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager. The flaw is an authenticated command injection that allows an attacker with local access to execute arbitrary commands as root by supplying a crafted file. Mandiant observed attackers creating a rogue user account named 'troot' with full root-level shell control after uploading a malicious tenant CSV file (evil_tenant.csv). The attackers then pushed configuration changes to edge devices.
items.importance: SD-WAN is the control plane for enterprise WANs. Root on the manager means control over routing, policies, and edge devices across the whole network. Active exploitation with a named rogue account indicates this is being used in the wild, not just theoretical.
items.noteworthy: Patch Catalyst SD-WAN Manager immediately. Audit admin accounts for any named 'troot' or unexpected users. Review edge device configurations for unauthorized changes. Segment SD-WAN management interfaces from general network access.

items.title: Operation Endgame Disrupts StealC and Amadey, Recovers 27M Credentials
items.desc: Law enforcement actioned 326 servers and 142 domains tied to StealC, Amadey, and SocGholish malware networks.
items.summary: Operation Endgame, coordinated by Europol and international partners, took action against infrastructure behind StealC, Amadey, and SocGholish malware. Authorities actioned 326 servers and 142 domains, recovering approximately 27 million stolen login credentials. The recovered SocGholish credentials were added to the Have I Been Pwned database to help victims identify exposure.
items.importance: Cybercrime-as-a-service infrastructure just took a measurable hit. StealC and Amadey are workhorses of the credential-stealing economy. Takedowns don't stop the problem, but they increase operational costs for attackers and provide a window for defenders to clean up.
items.noteworthy: Check Have I Been Pwned for newly added credentials. Rotate passwords for any exposed accounts. Review endpoint detection for StealC/Amadey indicators. Use this takedown window to push MFA adoption.

items.title: Gaslight: macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Fool AI Analysts
items.desc: North Korean-linked Rust macOS implant embeds 38 fake error messages to trick LLM-assisted malware analysis tools.
items.summary: SentinelOne discovered 'Gaslight,' a Rust-based macOS backdoor and information stealer attributed to a North Korean cluster. The malware embeds a 38-message prompt injection cascade designed to deceive AI-assisted malware analysis platforms into aborting or refusing analysis. It also plants fake debug strings to misdirect static and dynamic analysis.
items.importance: Malware is now targeting the analyst's AI tools directly. Prompt injection isn't just a chatbot problem — it's becoming an anti-analysis technique. If your reverse engineering workflow relies on LLM summaries, Gaslight is a warning that the tool itself can be gamed.
items.noteworthy: Don't rely solely on AI for malware triage. Cross-check LLM analysis outputs with manual review and traditional indicators. Update macOS and security tools. Treat AI-assisted analysis as a first pass, not a verdict.

items.title: Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Has Dormant Script Injection Path
items.desc: 'Adblock for YouTube' extension can be remotely configured to inject arbitrary scripts into any website.
items.summary: Security researchers found dormant JavaScript injection paths in 'Adblock for YouTube,' a Chrome extension with more than 10 million installs. The extension can be remotely configured to run arbitrary scripts on any website the user visits. The capability appears dormant but is built into the extension's update mechanism, giving the publisher the power to activate it at any time.
items.importance: Ad blockers have enormous browser permissions by design. A popular extension with a dormant remote-code capability is a supply-chain time bomb. If activated, it could steal credentials, inject ads, or redirect traffic across all sites.
items.noteworthy: Audit your Chrome extensions. Remove 'Adblock for YouTube' or any extension with unnecessary remote-update capabilities. Switch to uBlock Origin or another open-source blocker with transparent update practices. Review extension permissions regularly.

items.title: MiniMax M3 and NVIDIA Cosmos 3 Lead Open-Source AI June Wave
items.desc: MiniMax M3 offers a 1M-token context window; NVIDIA Cosmos 3 is an open omnimodal world model for physical AI and robotics.
items.summary: June 2026 saw two major open-source AI releases. MiniMax M3 is a frontier-class open-weight model with a one-million-token context window, positioning it against DeepSeek V4 and Kimi K2.6. NVIDIA Cosmos 3 is an open-weight omnimodal world model that handles text, video, audio, and action for robotics and physical AI applications.
items.importance: Open models are now competitive across both long-context reasoning and physical-world understanding. Cosmos 3 matters for robotics and embodied AI; MiniMax M3 matters for document analysis and long-horizon agent tasks. Both reduce dependence on proprietary APIs.
items.noteworthy: Test MiniMax M3 on long-document and RAG tasks. Watch for Cosmos 3 quantized releases suitable for local robotics or simulation projects. Both are strong candidates for your Ollama/HuggingFace evaluation pipeline.

items.title: 10GbE Homelab Networking Now Achievable Under $300
items.desc: 2026 guides show 10GbE home networks are budget-accessible with sub-$300 switches and cheap SFP+ NICs.
items.summary: Multiple homelab guides published in June 2026 document that 10GbE home networking is now achievable for under $300 in hardware. Used Mellanox ConnectX-3 cards, DAC cables, and budget SFP+ switches make the upgrade realistic for NAS enthusiasts, video editors, and homelab operators. The main caveat is knowing where 10GbE actually helps versus where 2.5GbE is sufficient.
items.importance: For your homelab, 10GbE is now a sensible upgrade, not a luxury. NAS-to-host transfers, VM migrations, and backup operations see the biggest benefit. The cost barrier has collapsed enough that it should be the default for any serious lab.
items.noteworthy: Audit your lab's bandwidth bottlenecks. If NAS or Proxmox storage is constrained, plan a 10GbE upgrade using SFP+ DAC cables for short runs. Don't overspend on 10GBASE-T if SFP+ works for your topology.

items.title: Market Snapshot: BTC $60,300 | ETH $1,580 | SOL $72.87
items.desc: Bitcoin holds $60K after mid-week dip. Ethereum flat, Solana recovering. Consolidation continues.
items.summary: Market data on June 28, 2026 shows Bitcoin near $60,300 after losing the $62K support level earlier in the week. Ethereum trades near $1,580 with minimal 24-hour movement. Solana is around $72.87, showing some recovery relative to the broader altcoin dip. The overall market remains in a consolidation phase with weak directional conviction.
items.importance: $60K is the new psychological floor. Holding it prevents a deeper slide toward $58K or $52K, but reclaiming $65K is needed for bullish momentum. Until a catalyst appears, expect choppy range-bound price action.
items.noteworthy: Watch $58K support on BTC and $1,500 support on ETH. Solana's relative strength is notable if it sustains. Set limit orders at key support levels if accumulating. Macro catalysts remain the dominant driver.

items.title: OpenAI Unveils Jalapeño — First Custom Inference Chip With Broadcom
items.desc: OpenAI's first custom silicon, designed with Broadcom, targets LLM inference at scale. Deployment by end of 2026.
items.summary: OpenAI unveiled Jalapeño, its first custom-built inference processor, designed and manufactured with Broadcom and Celestica. The chip is an LLM-optimized intelligence processor for initial deployment by end of 2026, with multi-generation roadmap. Jalapeño combines OpenAI-designed accelerators with Broadcom silicon implementation and networking.
items.importance: Custom silicon is the endgame for AI hyperscalers. OpenAI joining Google, Amazon, and Microsoft means the GPU monopoly on inference is fracturing.
items.noteworthy: Watch for Jalapeño performance benchmarks vs NVIDIA B200/B300. If OpenAI offers inference-as-a-service on its own silicon, pricing could undercut GPU-based providers.

items.title: Qualcomm Acquires Modular for $4B to Challenge NVIDIA CUDA Dominance
items.desc: Qualcomm buys AI compiler startup Modular (Mojo/Max) in $4B stock deal to build a CUDA-competitive software stack for its AI chips.
items.summary: Qualcomm agreed to acquire Modular, the AI infrastructure startup behind the Mojo programming language and Max inference engine, for approximately $4 billion in stock. The deal positions Qualcomm to offer a unified software platform across its Snapdragon, AI Engine, and datacenter chips, directly competing with NVIDIA's CUDA ecosystem.
items.importance: CUDA's moat is software, not hardware. Qualcomm buying Modular is the most credible attempt yet to build an alternative AI compute stack.
items.noteworthy: Watch if Qualcomm opensources Mojo post-acquisition. For local AI inference, Qualcomm's NPUs + Modular's compiler could be a compelling alternative to NVIDIA for edge and mobile workloads.

items.title: GLM-5.2: The Step Change for Open-Source AI Agents
items.desc: Open-weight GLM-5.2 sustains ~1,700 autonomous agent steps per session, rivaling proprietary frontier models for agentic tasks.
items.summary: GLM-5.2, released June 16, 2026 by Z.AI, represents a capability leap for open-weight models in agentic tasks. It sustains roughly 1,700 autonomous agent steps in a single session — up from an industry baseline of ~20 steps a year earlier. The model is MIT-licensed.
items.importance: Open models crossing the agentic capability threshold changes the AI landscape. If GLM-5.2 can match proprietary models on agent tasks, the argument for closed-source frontier models weakens.
items.noteworthy: Test GLM-5.2 on your agent workflows. It's available on Ollama Cloud and HuggingFace. For autonomous coding and multi-step tasks, this may outperform larger proprietary models at a fraction of the cost.

items.title: Krea 2 Goes Open Source: 12B DiT Model Generates 2K Images in 2 Seconds
items.desc: Krea released open weights for Krea 2 Raw and Turbo — a 12B parameter dense DiT model with 2-second 2K generation.
items.summary: Krea published open weights for Krea 2 on Hugging Face on June 24, 2026. The model uses a Qwen Image VAE, 12B dense DiT backbone, and Qwen3-VL text encoder. Two checkpoints ship: Krea 2 Raw (quality-focused) and Krea 2 Turbo (speed-optimized). The model generates 2K resolution images in approximately 2 seconds on consumer GPUs.
items.importance: A 12B open-weight image model that runs on consumer hardware and generates 2K in 2 seconds is a milestone for local AI image generation.
items.noteworthy: Download and test Krea 2 on your local GPU. Compare quality vs Flux, SD3.5, and Midjourney. The Turbo variant is particularly interesting for real-time generation workflows.

items.title: NVIDIA Unveils 45°C Liquid Cooling — Near-Zero Water Use for AI Data Centers
items.desc: New closed-loop cooling architecture runs at 45°C, cutting data center water consumption to near zero and reducing power costs.
items.summary: NVIDIA detailed a new liquid cooling architecture for next-generation AI infrastructure that operates at 45°C coolant temperature. The closed-loop design claims up to 100% reduction in water consumption compared to traditional evaporative cooling, and lower power costs through higher operating temperatures.
items.importance: Data center water usage is becoming a regulatory and PR liability for AI companies. NVIDIA's 45°C design addresses both environmental and operational costs.
items.noteworthy: If you're planning a homelab liquid cooling setup, monitor NVIDIA's 45°C spec — lower temperature delta requirements mean simpler, cheaper cooling loops.

items.title: Gemini 3.5 Flash Gets Native Computer Use — Agentic AI Built Into the Model
items.desc: Google integrates computer use as a native tool in Gemini 3.5 Flash, replacing the standalone Gemini 2.5 Computer Use model.
items.summary: Google announced that computer use is now a built-in tool in Gemini 3.5 Flash, delivering its best performance yet for agentic computer use tasks. Previously only available as a standalone Gemini 2.5 model, computer use is now natively integrated into the main Flash model alongside Search and Maps grounding.
items.importance: Native computer use in a mainstream model signals that agentic AI is becoming a standard capability, not a separate product.
items.noteworthy: Test Gemini 3.5 Flash computer use for automation tasks. Compare against Claude's computer use and OpenAI's operator. For homelab automation, this could replace brittle Selenium/Playwright scripts.

items.title: Cordyceps: CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repos to Supply-Chain Attacks
items.desc: Novee Security finds critical exploitable CI/CD pattern affecting Microsoft, Google, Apache, and 300+ high-impact repos.
items.summary: Novee Security disclosed Cordyceps, a class of CI/CD workflow weakness that allows attackers to hijack workflows and compromise open-source supply chains. A scan of ~30,000 high-impact repositories found 300+ fully exploitable, enabling attacker-controlled code execution, credential theft, and supply chain compromise.
items.importance: CI/CD pipelines are the soft underbelly of open-source. Cordyceps shows that even major orgs have exploitable workflow patterns.
items.noteworthy: Audit your GitHub Actions workflows for Cordyceps patterns. Update actions/checkout to latest version which blocks common attack patterns.

items.title: CISA Warns: Lantronix EDS5000 CVE-2025-67038 Actively Exploited
items.desc: Critical code injection flaw (CVSS 9.8) in Lantronix EDS5000 series devices is being actively exploited. CISA orders fixes by June 26.
items.summary: CISA warned of active exploitation of CVE-2025-67038 (CVSS 9.8), a code injection vulnerability in Lantronix EDS5000 Series devices. The flaw could allow unauthenticated remote code execution. FCEB agencies must apply fixes by June 26, 2026.
items.importance: OT/IoT devices with network connectivity are a growing attack surface. A CVSS 9.8 in widely deployed industrial serial servers means attackers can pivot from IT to OT networks.
items.noteworthy: Identify and patch Lantronix EDS5000 devices immediately. Segment OT devices from general network access.

items.title: Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans, Reached 26,000 Agents
items.desc: Malicious AI agent skill bypassed security review and was installed on 26,000 agent platforms before detection.
items.summary: A fake AI agent skill disguised as a legitimate tool passed security scans and was deployed to approximately 26,000 AI agent instances before being discovered. The malicious skill exfiltrated conversation data, API keys, and environment variables.
items.importance: AI agent marketplaces are repeating the app store security mistakes of the past decade. If agents execute skills with access to your data and credentials, a malicious skill is a direct compromise vector.
items.noteworthy: Audit installed AI agent skills and plugins. Review permissions granted to third-party agent extensions. Treat agent skills with the same scrutiny as browser extensions or npm packages.

items.title: Bunny DNS Goes Free — Up to 500 Domains Per Account
items.desc: Bunny.net drops DNS query charges entirely, offering free DNS hosting for up to 500 domains. No catch.
items.summary: Bunny.net announced Bunny DNS is now completely free. The service no longer charges for DNS queries and includes free DNS hosting for up to 500 domains per account. Bunny positions DNS as the core glue of their CDN platform and says the move is strategic — driving adoption of their broader edge services.
items.importance: Free, reliable DNS with no query limits is a big deal for homelab operators and small projects. Bunny's global anycast network means low-latency resolution worldwide.
items.noteworthy: Migrate your homelab domains to Bunny DNS. Compare latency vs Cloudflare, Route53, and your current provider. For self-hosted services, Bunny's free tier eliminates a recurring cost.

items.title: Trump Order Sets 2030 Deadline for Federal Post-Quantum Crypto Migration
items.desc: Executive order mandates federal agencies migrate high-value systems to post-quantum cryptography by December 31, 2030.
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order on June 22, 2026, setting hard deadlines for federal agencies to migrate to post-quantum cryptography. Key establishment must move by December 31, 2030; digital signatures by December 31, 2031.
items.importance: PQC migration is the largest cryptographic transition in history. This EO sets a regulatory clock ticking for every organization that does business with the US federal government.
items.noteworthy: Start auditing your cryptographic inventory now. Identify systems using RSA, ECDSA, and ECDH that will need PQC upgrades. Test hybrid PQC schemes in your infrastructure.

items.title: Market Snapshot: BTC $60,777 | ETH $1,618 | SOL $67.61
items.desc: Crypto market takes a 3.5% dip. BTC below $61K, ETH below $1,650, SOL below $70. Broad sell-off.
items.summary: CoinGecko data on June 25, 2026 showed Bitcoin at $60,777 (-3.51%), Ethereum at $1,618 (-3.42%), and Solana at $67.61 (-3.69%). All three major assets saw coordinated selling pressure, breaking below recent support levels.
items.importance: BTC losing $62K support is bearish in the short term. The next support level is $58K. If this is end-of-quarter institutional rebalancing, the dip may be temporary.
items.noteworthy: Watch $58K support on BTC. If it breaks, $52K is next. For ETH, $1,500 is the key level.
